Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

Thomas Nagel

b. 1937

What it is like to be a subject: the irreducibility of point of view.

Thomas Nagel’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a sustained attempt to show that the mind is not a local anomaly to be absorbed into an otherwise complete physical picture, but a datum that forces revision of what counts as an adequate account of nature. His central claim is not merely that consciousness is hard to explain. It is that the dominant form of modern objectivity was historically constituted by subtracting subjectivity from the physical world, and that one cannot later recover consciousness by extending that same objectifying method without transforming it. In this sense, Nagel is neither a Cartesian dualist nor a standard physicalist. He is a realist about consciousness, an anti-reductionist about phenomenal subjectivity, a critic of functionalist and behaviorist analyses of mind, and, in his later work, an advocate of an enlarged naturalism tending toward neutral monism, dual-aspect theory, panpsychist pressure, or natural teleology. He wants the mind placed in nature, but not by explaining it away.

The report below reconstructs a comprehensive “Nagelian” philosophy of mind out of his major writings: “Brain Bisection and the Unity of Consciousness,” “What Is It Like to Be a Bat?,” “Panpsychism,” “Subjective and Objective,” The View from Nowhere, “Consciousness and Objective Reality,” “The Psychophysical Nexus,” “Science and the Mind-Body Problem,” and Mind and Cosmos.

1. Historical position: Nagel in the development of philosophy of mind

Nagel enters philosophy of mind at a pivotal moment. Early modern science, on Nagel’s own reconstruction, made progress by dividing reality into mathematically tractable primary qualities—shape, size, motion, spatiotemporal structure—and subjective appearances such as color, taste, and felt qualities, which were assigned to the mind. Galileo and Descartes, in this picture, made the physical world scientifically manageable by excluding the subjective from the official subject matter of physics. The later mind-body problem is therefore not accidental: it is the return of what modern physical science had methodologically bracketed at its birth.

In twentieth-century analytic philosophy, the main physicalist strategies before and around Nagel were behaviorism, mind-brain identity theory, and functionalism. Behaviorism tried to understand the mental through behavior or behavioral criteria; Nagel’s diagnosis is that this leaves out the first-person inner standpoint of the conscious subject. Identity theory, associated in its modern form with U. T. Place, Herbert Feigl, and J. J. C. Smart, held that mental states and processes are brain states and processes, not merely correlated with them. Functionalism, which became fully articulated and widely endorsed in the last third of the twentieth century, held that a mental state is individuated by the role it plays in a cognitive system: its causal relations to stimuli, behavior, and other mental states.

Nagel’s importance is that he shifted the center of gravity from “Can mental discourse be translated into physical, behavioral, or functional discourse?” to “Can any objective description capture what conscious experience is from the inside?” His 1974 paper “What Is It Like to Be a Bat?” became one of the decisive texts in restoring phenomenal consciousness to the center of philosophy, psychology, and neuroscience; Oxford’s recent abstract of the essay explicitly describes it as influential in drawing attention to consciousness as a central problem across those disciplines and in encouraging serious study of non-human consciousness.

This historical role is hard to overstate. Contemporary philosophy of mind now treats consciousness as arguably the central issue in theorizing about mind, and Nagel’s “what it is like” formulation remains one of the standard ways of defining phenomenal consciousness. The later knowledge argument, the explanatory gap, zombie arguments, and Chalmers’s “hard problem” all inherit or reformulate Nagelian pressure: a complete structural, functional, or physical account seems to leave open the question of why there is experience at all, or why it has the qualitative character it has.

Nagel should therefore be placed in the transition from mid-century reductionist optimism to late twentieth-century consciousness realism. He is not merely one critic among others; he is one of the figures who made the subjective character of experience unavoidable for analytic philosophy of mind.

2. The core Nagelian datum: “what it is like”

Nagel’s most famous formulation is that an organism is conscious if and only if there is something it is like to be that organism. This is not a theory of consciousness in the sense of a mechanism. It is a criterion for isolating the explanandum: the thing any adequate philosophy of mind must account for. Consciousness, for Nagel, is not first of all reportability, wakefulness, self-consciousness, information-processing, or behavioral flexibility. Those may matter, but the central datum is subjective character: experience as lived from a point of view.

This subjective character is not captured by familiar reductive analyses. Nagel explicitly says it is not analyzable in terms of functional states, intentional states, or causal roles, since those descriptions appear logically compatible with the absence of experience. He does not deny that conscious events have causes, effects, functions, or neural bases. His point is stricter: causal and functional description does not by itself analyze or disclose what makes an experience an experience.

The bat example is designed to make this point vivid. Bats are mammals, and Nagel assumes it is reasonable to think they have conscious experience. But their echolocatory mode of perception is deeply alien to us. We can imagine flying, hanging upside down, catching insects, or navigating by sound; but that tells us only what it would be like for us to behave as bats behave. It does not tell us what it is like for a bat to be a bat. The problem is not a shortage of scientific information about bats. It is that a certain kind of subjective fact may be inaccessible from our form of life and our sensory imagination.

A subtle point matters here. Nagel’s claim is not merely that each individual’s experiences are private. He explicitly distinguishes subjective facts from merely private facts. A point of view may be a type, not an individual possession. Humans can often understand what other humans experience because we share enough of the relevant standpoint. The difficulty increases as the form of consciousness becomes more alien. Thus the problem is not solipsism; it is perspectival access.

The result is a form of realism about subjective facts. Nagel argues that reflection on bats leads us to recognize that there may be facts not expressible in human language or fully comprehensible by human concepts. This is not anti-realism. It is almost the opposite: reality may outrun the conceptual capacities of a particular species. Subjective facts are real even when not capturable by the most detached human description.

3. Subjectivity and objectivity: the master distinction

Nagel’s philosophy of mind is part of a broader philosophical project about subjective and objective standpoints. The View from Nowhere is explicitly concerned with how to combine the perspective of a particular person inside the world with an objective view of that same world. The book generalizes what the bat paper had shown in the case of consciousness: human beings are both located subjects and creatures capable of detachment, abstraction, and objectivity.

For Nagel, objectivity is not simply “truth” as opposed to illusion. It is a direction of thought: one moves away from a local, species-specific, or individual standpoint toward descriptions that are less dependent on the peculiarities of the observer. This works well for ordinary physical reductions. For example, one can move from the human experience of heat to molecular motion, or from the appearance of sound to wave phenomena. The more objective description explains why things appear as they do, while the appearances can be treated as effects in perceivers.

Consciousness breaks this model. In ordinary physical reduction, one can leave the appearance behind because the appearance is not the thing being reduced. But in the case of experience, the appearance from the subject’s point of view is precisely what must be explained. If we remove the bat’s point of view, Nagel asks, what remains of what it is like to be a bat? If the subjective character of experience is fully comprehensible only from a point of view, then moving toward greater objectivity may take us farther from the phenomenon rather than closer to it.

This is Nagel’s deepest objection to reductionism. Reductionism is not wrong because science is bad or because the brain is irrelevant. It is wrong, or at least currently unintelligible, because the standard model of objective explanation depends on a maneuver that is illegitimate for consciousness: subtracting the subject’s standpoint from the explanandum.

4. Nagel against behaviorism, identity theory, and functionalism

Nagel’s criticism of behaviorism is direct: behavior supplies evidence for mental states, especially in other beings, but it is not what makes those states conscious. A behavioral analysis leaves out the inner mental state itself—the way sugar tastes, the way red looks, the way pain feels. This is why behaviorism cannot solve the mind-body problem; it changes the subject from experience to its outward criteria or manifestations.

His criticism of identity theory is more subtle. Nagel does not simply say that mental states cannot be brain states. He says we currently lack an intelligible account of how a brain state could be identical with a subjective experience. In other theoretical identities, such as water being H₂O, the sensory appearances used to identify water are not part of the intrinsic nature of water itself. But in the case of pain, taste, color-experience, or conscious thought, the “appearance” is not external to the phenomenon. The felt character is what has to be explained.

Kripke’s modal challenge intensifies this. If pain is identical with a neural state, then the identity should be necessary, as water = H₂O is necessary if true. But it seems conceivable that the neural state could occur without the experience, or that the experience could occur with a different physical realization. Nagel accepts the force of this appearance but does not rest content with property dualism. His later view is that the apparent contingency of mind-brain correlations may reflect the inadequacy of our concepts: the connection may be necessary but nonconceptual, hidden from us because we lack the deeper terms in which both the mental and physical aspects can be understood.

Functionalism fares better than behaviorism because it acknowledges internal organization, causal role, and systemic relations. But Nagel still thinks it fails as a full theory of mind. In “Consciousness and Objective Reality,” he says functionalism is part of the truth but not adequate: conscious states have functional roles and physiological bases, but also subjective experiential qualities that are not equivalent to those roles and not analyzable in physical descriptions of their neural bases.

So Nagel’s position is not that physical, functional, and behavioral facts are irrelevant. His position is that they are incomplete. They describe the organism from the outside, or describe the role of mental states within the organism, but they do not yet explain why those states are lived from the inside.

5. The mind-body problem is not merely epistemological

A common misreading is that Nagel makes only an epistemological point: we cannot know what bat experience is like, but perhaps a completed science could. Nagel’s argument is more radical. He is concerned with what kind of fact experience is and what kind of description could capture it. The epistemic problem—our inability to imagine bat experience—is evidence for a deeper conceptual and metaphysical issue: subjective facts seem essentially tied to types of point of view.

This is why Nagel says that we cannot understand the hypothesis that experience is captured by a physical description unless we first understand the more basic idea that experiences have an objective nature, or that objective processes can have subjective nature. The challenge is not just to find the neural correlate of consciousness, but to explain how the same reality can be both objectively describable and subjectively lived.

This distinction also explains why Nagel is not a simple mysterian. He does not conclude that consciousness is forever inexplicable. He says that no currently available concept of reduction applies, while leaving open the possibility of a new theoretical form in the distant intellectual future.

6. Objective phenomenology: Nagel’s constructive proposal

Nagel’s most underappreciated constructive idea is “objective phenomenology.” At the end of “What Is It Like to Be a Bat?” he proposes that we may need to pursue an objective understanding of the mental in its own right, temporarily setting aside the mind-brain relation. The goal would be to form new concepts and methods that describe subjective character in a way not wholly dependent on empathy or imagination. Such a phenomenology would not capture everything, but it might describe at least part of the subjective character of experiences in terms comprehensible to beings incapable of having those experiences.

This is crucial for extracting a philosophy of psychology from Nagel. Psychology, on this view, cannot be reduced to behavior, information processing, or neurophysiology. But neither should it remain a merely introspective or literary discipline. It needs disciplined concepts for describing structures of experience, comparative forms of consciousness, sensory modalities, affective life, attention, embodiment, temporality, and agency. Nagel’s objective phenomenology is therefore a bridge project: it tries to make the subjective scientifically and philosophically tractable without eliminating its first-person character.

This also connects Nagel to later debates about first-person and third-person data. Contemporary consciousness studies often recognizes that an adequate descriptive project requires both trained first-person attention to experience and third-person empirical data from psychology, neuroscience, and pathology. That methodological pluralism is very much in the spirit of Nagel’s demand that subjective character be faced directly rather than redescribed away.

7. Animal minds and comparative psychology

Nagel’s bat argument helped legitimize animal consciousness as a philosophical and scientific problem. It does not say that animal minds are unknowable in every respect. Rather, it says that the more a creature’s form of life differs from ours, the less we can rely on imaginative projection from our own case. This is a warning against both anthropomorphism and anthropocentric denial. We should neither assume that bat experience is just a modified human experience nor deny its reality because we cannot inhabit it.

For comparative psychology, the Nagelian lesson is that behavioral and neurophysiological evidence can support attributions of consciousness, but understanding the subjective character of another species’ experience is a further task. The point is not simply “animals have qualia.” It is that different forms of life may disclose different phenomenal worlds, different modes of spatiality, salience, temporality, bodily orientation, and environmental meaning.

This gives Nagel’s thought an ethological dimension. Consciousness is not a generic glow added to organisms. It is a mode of being in a world from a specific standpoint. A bat’s world, an octopus’s world, a dog’s olfactory world, and a human linguistic-social world may all be conscious worlds, but their subjective structures may differ in ways not recoverable by simple analogy.

8. Unity of consciousness and the split-brain problem

Nagel’s “Brain Bisection and the Unity of Consciousness” shows that his philosophy of mind is not limited to qualia. He is also concerned with the unity of the subject. Split-brain cases challenge the ordinary assumption that a person has exactly one mind, one unified field of consciousness, and one integrated subject of experience. Nagel argues that if a phenomenal feature of mind is left unaccounted for by physical theory, we cannot postpone it as if we were studying some external phenomenon; the mind itself is what we are trying to understand.

In split-brain patients, ordinary life often displays enough integration that treating the patient as a single person seems natural. Yet experimental conditions reveal dissociations between hemispheres that make the two-minds hypothesis tempting. Nagel argues that both the one-mind and two-minds hypotheses face deep difficulties. The integration of ordinary behavior supports unity; the experimental dissociation supports division.

His most radical conclusion is that these cases may not admit any answer in terms of a whole number of minds. They fall between ordinary intact persons and two separate individuals. If the decision about the number of minds associated with a brain is largely arbitrary, then the original question loses its grip.

This has major consequences for a Nagelian philosophy of psychology. The unity of consciousness is not a trivial given. It is a structural feature of experience that can break down, fragment, or become indeterminate. Psychology must therefore treat “the subject” not as a simple metaphysical atom but as a complex organization of experiential, cognitive, bodily, and neural integration. Nagel does not abandon subjectivity; he complicates the assumption that every episode of conscious activity belongs straightforwardly to one countable mind.

9. Intentionality, artificial intelligence, and the priority of consciousness

Nagel’s theory of mind also includes intentionality: the directedness of thought, perception, belief, desire, and language toward objects or states of affairs. In “Consciousness and Objective Reality,” he says the problem of intentionality links mind-brain debates with questions about artificial intelligence and the possibility of ascribing mental states to computers. But he also says consciousness is the more fundamental problem. Pure sensation does not by itself guarantee intentionality, yet true intentionality cannot occur in a being incapable of consciousness.

This gives Nagel a strong anti-computationalist tendency. He allows that we can assign meaning to the outputs of an unconscious computer, just as we can assign meaning to marks in a book. But on his view, the computer itself does not mean or intend anything unless there is consciousness. Therefore, before we can fruitfully answer whether artificial systems could have minds, we need a general theory of why biological organisms have minds at all.

This is not a cheap denial that machines could ever be conscious. Nagel’s claim is conditional and methodological: if functionalism were true, artificial mind would become a relatively intelligible engineering question about realizing the right causal organization. But because functionalism is incomplete, artificial consciousness cannot be settled by functional duplication alone. What matters is whether the system has a subjective point of view, and we do not yet understand the conditions under which such a point of view exists.

10. Cognition, reason, and the mind’s access to objectivity

Nagel’s philosophy of mind expands beyond sensation into cognition and reason. In Mind and Cosmos, he argues that thought and reasoning present a problem beyond the irreducibility of consciousness. Consciousness is subjective, but reason is remarkable because it can transcend subjectivity: it aims at objective truth, logical validity, mathematical necessity, and practical or moral correctness.

Nagel assumes that attributing knowledge to a computer is metaphorical and that higher cognitive capacities can be possessed only by beings that also have consciousness. More importantly, he argues that reasoning is governed by standards independent of the thinker’s beliefs or community. When we reason, we take ourselves not merely to be caused to believe, but to be responding to reasons that can be correct or incorrect.

This gives Nagel’s philosophy of psychology a normative dimension. A psychology of belief formation, inference, and decision cannot be complete if it treats reasoning only as causal processing or evolutionary adaptation. It must also account for the subject’s rational relation to truth. Nagel’s broader anti-reductionism therefore concerns not only phenomenal consciousness but also the objectivity of thought, the validity of logic, and the reality of value.

11. The psychophysical nexus: necessary connection without reduction

Nagel is deeply dissatisfied with both reductive physicalism and property dualism. In “The Psychophysical Nexus,” he explores an approach distinct from dualism and from conceptual reductions of the mental via causal-behaviorist or functionalist analysis. The key idea is that subjective phenomenological features are perfectly real and not reducible to anything else, but their systematic relations to neurophysiology may nevertheless be necessary rather than contingent.

This is perhaps the most precise formulation of Nagel’s mature position. He wants to avoid brute dualism, where mental properties are simply attached to physical states by unexplained laws. But he also rejects the idea that mental concepts can be analyzed away into physical or functional concepts. The hoped-for theory would reveal a necessary connection between brain and experience that is not available through our current conceptual scheme.

In “Science and the Mind-Body Problem,” Nagel gives the clearest version of this idea. Both the physical brain state and the subjective experience may be partial descriptions of a deeper underlying reality, one that manifests from the inside as experience and from the outside as brain process. That would be a form of monism—he compares it to Spinoza—that is neither idealist nor materialist.

This is a dual-aspect or neutral-monist tendency. The world is not divided into two substances, mind and matter. Nor is mind reduced to matter as currently conceived. Instead, the physical and the mental are two aspects, manifestations, or incomplete descriptions of a more fundamental reality for which we do not yet possess adequate concepts.

12. Panpsychism and the pressure from composition

Nagel’s “Panpsychism” is often misunderstood as a simple endorsement of the view that electrons have little minds. His actual argument is more conditional and diagnostic. He defines panpsychism as the view that the basic physical constituents of the universe have mental properties, whether or not they are parts of organisms. He then argues that this view appears to follow from premises each of which seems plausible: living organisms are complex material systems; no nonmaterial ingredients are added when matter becomes part of a brain; and ordinary mental states are not physical, behavioral, or physiological properties and are not implied by physical properties alone.

The pressure is clear. If organisms are made entirely of matter, and if consciousness is not reducible to the physical properties of that matter, then either consciousness emerges radically from nonconscious matter, or matter itself must have more in it than physics describes. Panpsychism is one way to avoid brute emergence by locating proto-mental or mental properties at the base of nature.

Nagel does not solve the combination problem: how micro-level mental properties combine into unified macro-level consciousness. But he treats panpsychism as a serious metaphysical pressure generated by anti-reductionism and material composition. In later work, this pressure reappears as neutral monism: the basic constituents of the universe may have properties that explain both physical and mental character, rather than being purely physical as currently understood.

13. Mind and Cosmos: from philosophy of mind to philosophy of nature

In Mind and Cosmos, Nagel radicalizes the implications of the mind-body problem. He argues that the problem is not merely local—not just a puzzle about the relation between mind, brain, and behavior in animals—but one that invades our understanding of the entire cosmos and its history. The physical sciences and evolutionary biology, he says, cannot remain insulated from it.

The central claim is that if reductionism fails, and if the mental cannot be denied, then materialist naturalism is false or at least radically incomplete. Mind is not an accident, afterthought, or add-on, but a basic aspect of nature. Consciousness is, for Nagel, the clearest obstacle to a comprehensive naturalism relying only on physical science.

Nagel’s argument about evolution is not that evolutionary biology is false in its ordinary empirical claims about descent, adaptation, or selection. His complaint is philosophical: a purely physical evolutionary story may explain the appearance of complex organisms with nervous systems, but if consciousness is not reducible to the physical, then such a story does not explain why conscious points of view appear. It identifies causal conditions but does not explain why those conditions produce subjective life.

His positive suggestion is an expanded naturalism. Philosophy cannot itself generate the needed scientific explanations, but it can point out their absence and the obstacles facing current materials. Nagel imagines a long-term project integrating neurophysiological, evolutionary, and metaphysical inquiry, eventually allowing us to see conscious organisms as specific expressions of both the physical and mental character of the universe.

This is the most controversial part of Nagel’s thought. Critics such as John Dupré argue that Nagel places excessive explanatory demands on evolution, treats materialism too closely as reductionism, and offers teleological laws without empirical evidence beyond their usefulness in making consciousness, reason, and value more expected.

The criticism is serious. But even if one rejects Nagel’s teleological speculation, his underlying challenge remains: a philosophy of nature that includes organisms, brains, behavior, and information-processing but leaves out subjective points of view has not yet explained mind.

14. Reconstructing a comprehensive Nagelian philosophy of mind

A systematic Nagelian philosophy of mind can be extracted as a set of connected theses.

First, phenomenal realism: conscious experience is real. Its reality is not exhausted by behavioral dispositions, functional organization, neural processes, or third-person descriptions. Any adequate account of mind must explain the subjective character of experience, not bypass it.

Second, the point-of-view thesis: every conscious experience is essentially connected to a standpoint. This standpoint need not be private to one individual; it may be a species-level or form-of-life standpoint. But some facts about experience are graspable only from sufficiently similar points of view.

Third, the anti-substitution thesis: behavior, function, intentional role, and neural realization are not substitutes for phenomenology. They may be necessary for a complete psychology, but they do not by themselves give the subjective character of experience.

Fourth, the objectivity diagnosis: modern physical objectivity was achieved by abstracting away from subjectivity. That abstraction is legitimate for many purposes, but it becomes problematic when the subject matter is subjectivity itself.

Fifth, the incompleteness of current physicalism: physicalism, if understood as the thesis that everything is physical or ultimately fixed by the physical, lacks an intelligible account of consciousness unless it can explain how objective processes have subjective character. Contemporary usage often treats “physicalism” and “materialism” as interchangeable, but Nagel’s main target is reductive materialist naturalism: the idea that physical science as presently conceived can ultimately account for everything real.

Sixth, anti-dualist monism: Nagel does not want Cartesian dualism or brute property dualism. He seeks a unified world picture in which mind and brain are necessarily connected, perhaps as two aspects of a deeper reality.

Seventh, objective phenomenology: before reducing mind to brain, we need better concepts for describing the mental in its own right. These concepts should make subjective structures partially communicable across differences in viewpoint without eliminating their perspectival character.

Eighth, consciousness-first intentionality: intentionality, meaning, belief, and desire depend on consciousness more deeply than functionalist or computational theories usually allow. An unconscious system may be interpreted as meaningful by us, but it does not itself mean or intend unless it has conscious mentality.

Ninth, rational transcendence: human cognition is not merely subjective representation; it aspires to objective truth. A complete theory of mind must account for the normativity of reason—correctness, validity, evidence, and truth—not only for the causal production of beliefs.

Tenth, fragile unity: the unity of consciousness and personhood is a real but conceptually difficult phenomenon. Split-brain cases show that our ordinary categories of “one mind” and “two minds” may fail under certain neuropsychological conditions.

Eleventh, expanded naturalism: because mind is real and not reducible to the physical as currently conceived, nature itself must be understood more broadly. This may require neutral monism, dual-aspect theory, panpsychist elements, teleological principles, or concepts not yet available.

15. Nagel’s philosophy of psychology

If we translate Nagel’s position into a philosophy of psychology, we get a distinctive research program.

Psychology must not be behaviorism. Behavior is evidence for mind and an expression of mind, but not the essence of mind. A psychology that studies only observable behavior omits the first-person phenomena it is supposed to explain.

Psychology must not be merely cognitive functionalism. Information-processing, representational role, and causal organization are important, but they do not by themselves explain why a state is conscious or what its experiential character is. Functionalism may be part of psychology, but not its metaphysical foundation.

Psychology must include phenomenology. It needs disciplined descriptions of felt quality, bodily orientation, affective tone, attention, perceptual field-structure, agency, temporality, and selfhood. Nagel’s objective phenomenology is not introspection in the casual sense; it is an attempt to develop shareable concepts for subjective structures.

Psychology must be comparative. Human consciousness is not the template of all consciousness. Animal minds may involve forms of subjectivity that humans can only partially understand. The task is to combine ethology, neuroscience, and phenomenological caution.

Psychology must be neurobiologically serious but not neuro-reductive. Nagel accepts that conscious processes are intimately connected with nervous systems and behavior, but he says our knowledge of those connections remains empirical and ad hoc rather than theoretically illuminating at the cosmological level.

Psychology must take pathologies of unity seriously. Split-brain cases, dissociation, blindsight-like phenomena, neglect, and other disruptions are not merely clinical curiosities; they test the conceptual structure of personhood, subjectivity, and consciousness. Nagel’s split-brain discussion shows that empirical findings can force revision of our most basic mental categories.

Psychology must include normativity. Human thought is not only a sequence of mental events but an activity answerable to reasons. A psychology of reasoning that treats logic and evidence merely as causal inputs misses the subject’s relation to truth.

16. Strengths of Nagel’s position

Nagel’s greatest strength is diagnostic. He explains why the mind-body problem is not just another hard scientific problem. The problem arises from the very structure of objective physical explanation. If physical science was formed by excluding subjective appearances, then the later demand to explain subjectivity in purely physical terms is not straightforward.

A second strength is his refusal of false alternatives. He does not accept eliminativism, because consciousness is too evident to deny. He does not accept standard reductionism, because it leaves out subjective character. But he also does not embrace crude dualism, because brute psychophysical correlation is explanatorily unsatisfying. This makes his position more subtle than many summaries suggest.

A third strength is methodological. Nagel gives philosophy of mind a disciplined humility. He insists that we may lack the concepts needed for a final theory, but this lack is not a license to ignore the phenomenon. The proper response is conceptual innovation: objective phenomenology, new psychophysical concepts, and perhaps a transformed natural science.

A fourth strength is his relevance to psychology and neuroscience. Nagel does not reject empirical science; he argues that empirical correlations between brain and consciousness are indispensable but not yet explanatory. That is an important distinction. Neuroscience can identify neural correlates and mechanisms, while philosophy asks what kind of account would make those correlations intelligible as consciousness.

17. Main objections and vulnerabilities

The first objection is that Nagel may overstate the gap between objective description and subjective character. Physicalists often argue that the gap reflects our concepts, not reality. Phenomenal concepts may present physical states in a special first-person way without implying nonphysical properties. The knowledge-argument literature contains versions of this response, including “new knowledge/old fact” and objectivist interpretations, according to which Mary or the bat case may show limits of objective understanding rather than falsity of physicalism.

The second objection is that Nagel’s demand for intelligibility may be too strong. Science often accepts explanatory identities or dependencies before they feel transparent. A physicalist may say that once we have mapped the causal, structural, and neural conditions of consciousness, the remaining “why is it like something?” question is a residue of intuition, not an additional fact.

The third objection is functionalist and representationalist. A functionalist may argue that Nagel isolates phenomenal character too sharply from cognitive role, discrimination, report, attention, and perceptual representation. A representationalist may argue that the phenomenal character of experience is exhausted by the way the world is represented to the subject, not by an extra intrinsic quale.

The fourth objection concerns panpsychism and neutral monism. Even if basic matter has proto-mental properties, it remains unclear how unified subjects arise from micro-level proto-experiences. Nagel’s split-brain paper makes this harder, not easier: unity is not a simple additive product. Panpsychism may avoid brute emergence only by facing a severe combination problem.

The fifth objection concerns Mind and Cosmos. Nagel’s later teleological suggestions are widely seen as speculative. Dupré’s criticism is that Nagel demands that evolution make consciousness, reason, and value not merely possible but expected, and then introduces teleological laws with little evidence beyond the explanatory role they would play.

These objections do not refute the core Nagelian challenge, but they show where the system is least developed: the positive theory remains programmatic. Nagel is strongest in showing that existing reductions fail; he is less successful in specifying the replacement theory.

18. Final synthesis

Nagel’s philosophy of mind can be summarized as a theory of the irreducible reality of points of view. Conscious beings are not merely physical systems with complex functional organization; they are centers of experience for whom the world appears in some way. This appearance is not an illusion to be subtracted but a fact to be included in reality. The problem is that modern objectivity, by design, abstracts from such appearances. Therefore, a complete philosophy of mind requires a new conception of objectivity wide enough to include subjectivity.

The resulting view is anti-reductionist but not anti-naturalist; realist but not dualist in the Cartesian sense; scientifically serious but critical of the assumption that current physical science has the right categories for everything real. It treats consciousness, intentionality, unity, reason, and value as interconnected aspects of minded life. It also treats psychology as a discipline that must integrate first-person phenomenology, third-person empirical study, comparative animal research, neurobiology, and normative analysis.

Nagel’s enduring contribution is not a finished theory but a constraint on any future theory: it must explain how the world contains subjects, not merely how physical systems behave. A theory of mind that cannot say why there is something it is like to be a creature has not yet explained the mind.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
